title: Working with External APIs
subtitle: Fetching data from other services in your app

sections.content:
Most real-world applications don't exist in isolation. They talk to other services to get data: weather apps call weather APIs, social media apps call authentication APIs, and e-commerce apps call payment APIs.

An **API** (Application Programming Interface) is just a URL that returns data instead of a web page. When you visit a website, your browser gets back HTML. When your code calls an API, it gets back **JSON** — structured data your program can use.

You already learned about APIs in Level 5. Now let's use one in real code.
sections.analogy: If your server is a restaurant kitchen, an external API is a supplier. You don't grow your own tomatoes — you call the supplier (API), place an order (request), and they deliver the goods (response). If the supplier is closed (API is down), you need a backup plan (error handling).

sections.instruction: Here's a route that fetches data from an external API. Read through it and notice the three key patterns: async/await, fetch, and try/catch.

blocks.label: Example: API route

sections.explanation: Three things to notice: (1) **async/await** lets you wait for external data without freezing your server. The 'await' keyword pauses this function until the data arrives, but other requests keep being handled. (2) **fetch** sends the HTTP request to the external API. (3) **try/catch** handles errors gracefully — if the joke API is down, your server still responds with a helpful error message instead of crashing.

sections.content: When working with external APIs, there are a few concepts that come up again and again. Understanding these will save you from common mistakes.
keyPoints: async/await — a way to wait for external data without blocking your server
API keys — authentication for paid or private APIs (keep these in .env!)
Rate limits — APIs limit how many requests you can make per minute
Error handling — always have a fallback if the API is down
sections.tip: Never put API keys directly in your code. Store them in a .env file (which you learned to .gitignore in Level 7). Access them with process.env.MY_API_KEY. If an API key ends up on GitHub, assume it's compromised and generate a new one.

sections.instruction: Try adding this API route to your Express server from Level 7. This calls a free joke API that doesn't require an API key — perfect for practice.

troubleshooting.solution: You need Node.js 18 or later for built-in fetch. Check with: node -v. If you're on an older version, install node-fetch: npm install node-fetch
Make sure you added the route before app.listen() in your app.js file, and restart your server.

sections.question: Why do we wrap the API call in a try/catch block?
options: JavaScript requires try/catch for all functions
To make the code run faster
To handle errors gracefully if the external API is down
To prevent the API from being called too often
sections.explanation: External APIs can fail for many reasons: the service is down, your internet drops, the API changed its format. Without try/catch, your server would crash. With it, you catch the error and send a friendly error message back to the user instead.

completionMessage: You can now fetch data from external APIs in your Express server! You understand async/await, error handling, and the key concepts around API integration. This is the last lesson in Level 9 — you've built a solid foundation of junior developer patterns.
